preamble.summary: A wisdom psalm that begins with the fool's declaration — 'There is no God' — and then surveys the resulting moral catastrophe. The LORD looks down from heaven to see if anyone acts wisely, and the verdict is universal: all have turned aside, all are corrupt, no one does good. The psalm names the wicked as those who 'devour my people like bread' and who shame the counsel of the poor. It ends with a longing cry for salvation to come from Zion.
preamble.remarkable: The fool's statement — 'there is no God' (ein Elohim) — is not philosophical atheism in the modern sense. The naval ('fool') is not making a metaphysical argument but a practical one: God is not present, God does not see, God will not act. This is functional atheism — living as though God's moral governance does not apply. The psalm's response is to describe God doing exactly what the fool denies: looking down (hishqif), observing (lir'ot), examining whether anyone seeks God (doresh et Elohim). The irony is precise: the fool says God is absent, and at that very moment God is watching the fool. The universal verdict — 'all have turned aside, together they are corrupt' (hakkol sar yachdav ne'elachu) — will be quoted by Paul in Romans 3:10-12 as evidence of universal human sinfulness.
preamble.friction: Verse 4 suddenly narrows from universal corruption to a specific accusation: 'those who devour my people as they eat bread.' The shift from 'all have turned aside' to 'those who devour my people' introduces a distinction the psalm seemed to deny — if all are corrupt, who are 'my people' being devoured? The phrase ammi ('my people') likely represents God's voice (as in a prophetic oracle) or the psalmist speaking as God's representative. The tension between universal corruption and the existence of an identifiable righteous remnant (the poor in v. 6, the generation of the righteous in v. 5) runs through the entire psalm without resolution.
preamble.connections: Psalm 53 is a near-duplicate of Psalm 14 with minor variations, including the substitution of Elohim for YHVH — evidence that the psalm circulated in two traditions. Paul's quotation in Romans 3:10-18 draws from the Greek Septuagint version, which expands the indictment significantly. The phrase 'salvation out of Zion' (yeshu'at Yisrael mi-Tsiyyon) connects to Psalm 53:6, Isaiah 59:20 ('A redeemer will come to Zion'), and Romans 11:26. The image of God looking down from heaven appears also in Psalm 33:13-14 and Genesis 11:5 (the Tower of Babel).

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֗חַ לְדָ֫וִ֥ד אָ֘מַ֤ר נָבָ֣ל בְּ֭לִבּוֹ אֵ֣ין אֱלֹהִ֑ים הִֽשְׁחִ֗יתוּ הִתְעִ֥יבוּ עֲלִילָ֗ה אֵ֣ין עֹֽשֵׂה־טֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God. They are corrupt, they have done abominable works, there is none that doeth good.
verses.rendering:
For the choirmaster. Of David.
The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.'
They are corrupt; their deeds are vile.
There is no one who does good.
translator_notes: naval does not mean 'fool' in the sense of intellectual deficiency. It describes a person who is morally senseless — who refuses to reckon with reality as it actually is. Nabal in 1 Samuel 25 is the narrative embodiment of this concept: a wealthy man who acts as though generosity and justice do not apply to him.

The plural 'they are corrupt' follows the singular 'the fool says' — the psalm moves from one fool's inner conviction to its social consequences. One person's functional atheism becomes a civilization's moral collapse.

key_terms.semantic_range: fool, senseless person, morally obtuse, one who disregards God and social bonds
key_terms.note: naval describes moral rather than intellectual failure. The naval is someone who knows better but refuses to act accordingly — who treats others with contempt because he has internally dismissed the moral order. The word carries connotations of disgrace and social corrosion.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה מִשָּׁמַיִם֮ הִשְׁקִ֢יף עַֽל־בְּנֵ֫י אָדָ֥ם לִ֭רְאוֹת הֲיֵ֣שׁ מַשְׂכִּ֑יל דֹּ֝רֵ֗שׁ אֶת־אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD looked down from heaven upon the children of men, to see if there were any that did understand, and seek God.
verses.rendering:
The LORD looks down from heaven
on the children of humanity
to see if there is anyone who acts wisely,
anyone who seeks God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The fool says 'there is no God' — and the next verse shows God looking directly at the fool. The verb hishqif ('to look down, to gaze from a height') implies deliberate observation from an elevated vantage point. God searches for two qualities: maskil ('one who acts wisely, who has insight') and doresh et Elohim ('one who seeks God'). The maskil is not merely intelligent but possesses practical wisdom — the ability to live in alignment with reality. The doresh is one who actively pursues God, who investigates, inquires, and seeks. The two terms together describe the opposite of the naval: where the fool dismisses God internally, the wise person seeks God actively. The psalm will report what God finds.
translator_notes: The verb darash ('to seek, inquire, investigate') implies effort and intentionality. Seeking God is not passive — it requires turning one's attention, inquiry, and desire toward God. The absence of seekers is as damning as the absence of doers.

verses.text_hebrew: הַכֹּ֥ל סָר֮ יַחְדָּ֢ו נֶ֫אֱלָ֥חוּ אֵ֤ין עֹשֵׂה־ט֑וֹב אֵ֝֗ין גַּם־אֶחָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: They are all gone aside, they are all together become filthy: there is none that doeth good, no, not one.
verses.rendering:
All have turned aside; together they have become corrupt.
There is no one who does good —
not even one.
verses.expanded_rendering: The divine investigation yields a devastating verdict. hakkol sar ('all have turned aside') — the verb sur means to deviate from a path, to swerve away from the direction one should be walking. yachdav ne'elachu ('together they have become foul, corrupt') — the verb alach means to turn sour, to become rancid, like milk that has gone bad. The metaphor is organic: humanity has not merely made wrong choices but has spoiled from the inside out. The final verdict repeats verse 1's conclusion with one devastating addition: ein oseh tov, ein gam echad ('there is no one who does good — not even one'). The gam echad ('even one') closes every escape route. The verdict is not 'most' or 'many' but all, without a single exception. This comprehensive indictment will reverberate through Jewish and Christian theology for millennia.
translator_notes: Paul quotes this verse in Romans 3:12, using it to establish universal human sinfulness before introducing justification by faith. In its original context, the psalm's universal indictment exists in tension with the 'generation of the righteous' mentioned in verse 5 — the psalm seems to both deny and assume the existence of a faithful remnant.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלֹ֥א יָדְע֗וּ כׇּל־פֹּ֥עֲלֵי אָ֑וֶן אֹכְלֵ֥י עַ֝מִּ֗י אָ֣כְלוּ לֶ֑חֶם יְ֝הוָ֗ה לֹ֣א קָרָֽאוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Have all the workers of iniquity no knowledge? who eat up my people as they eat bread, and call not upon the LORD.
verses.rendering:
Do they not know, all these workers of evil —
those who devour my people as they eat bread,
who do not call on the LORD?
verses.expanded_rendering: The tone shifts from cosmic survey to prophetic accusation. The question halo yade'u ('do they not know?') is rhetorical and incredulous — their ignorance is willful. The accusation is specific and brutal: okelei ammi akhelu lechem ('they eat my people as they eat bread'). The metaphor reduces the exploitation of God's people to something as casual as a meal. The oppressors consume the poor the way they consume their daily bread — without thought, without gratitude, without pause. The phrase ammi ('my people') injects God's voice into the psalm: these are God's people being devoured. The final clause — YHVH lo qara'u ('they do not call on the LORD') — connects their social predation to their religious vacancy. They devour the vulnerable because they never call on the God who defends them.
translator_notes: The transition from 'all have turned aside' (universal) to 'those who devour my people' (specific) implies a distinction within the universal corruption. The 'my people' language suggests a prophetic voice — God speaking through the psalmist about those who prey on the poor and faithful.

key_terms.semantic_range: iniquity, wickedness, trouble, sorrow, emptiness, deception
key_terms.note: aven carries a dual sense of moral evil and the emptiness or futility it produces. Workers of aven are people whose labor produces nothing but destruction — their effort is real, but its fruit is ruin.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֤ם ׀ פָּ֣חֲדוּ פָ֑חַד כִּֽי־אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים בְּד֣וֹר צַדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: There were they in great fear: for God is in the generation of the righteous.
verses.rendering:
There they are — utterly terrified!
For God is with the generation of the righteous.
verses.expanded_rendering: The scene shifts abruptly: sham pachadu fachad ('there they feared a fear'). The cognate accusative construction (fearing a fear) intensifies the terror — this is not mild anxiety but paralyzing dread. The word sham ('there') points as though the psalmist can see them in their panic. The cause of their terror: ki Elohim be-dor tsaddiq ('for God is in the generation of the righteous'). The very God whose existence the fool denied is present — not in the abstract but among a specific community, the dor tsaddiq ('righteous generation'). Despite the universal indictment of verse 3, there exists a group defined by their relationship to God. The wicked discover too late that God is real, present, and aligned with those they despised.
translator_notes: The phrase dor tsaddiq ('generation of the righteous') introduces a category that seems to contradict the universal corruption of verse 3. This tension is not resolved within the psalm — it reflects the biblical pattern of universal judgment coexisting with a faithful remnant.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲצַ֣ת עָנִ֣י תָבִ֑ישׁוּ כִּ֖י יְהוָ֣ה מַחְסֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye have shamed the counsel of the poor, because the LORD is his refuge.
verses.rendering:
You would shame the plans of the poor,
but the LORD is their refuge.
translator_notes: The shift to second person ('you would shame') makes the accusation direct and personal. The 'counsel' or 'plan' (etsah) of the poor likely refers to their trust in God — the wicked mock the poor for relying on a God who seems absent. The psalm's reply: the LORD is machsehu ('his refuge') — the same shelter-language used in Psalm 11:1.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֥י יִתֵּ֣ן מִצִּיּוֹן֮ יְשׁוּעַ֢ת יִשְׂרָ֫אֵ֥ל בְּשׁ֣וּב יְ֭הוָה שְׁב֣וּת עַמּ֑וֹ יָגֵ֥ל יַ֝עֲקֹ֗ב יִשְׂמַ֥ח יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Oh that the salvation of Israel were come out of Zion! when the LORD bringeth back the captivity of his people, Jacob shall rejoice, and Israel shall be glad.
verses.rendering:
Oh, that salvation for Israel would come from Zion!
When the LORD restores the fortunes of his people,
Jacob will rejoice; Israel will be glad.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends with a cry that reaches beyond the present into eschatological hope. mi yitten ('who will give?') is the Hebrew idiom for intense longing — 'if only!' The salvation longed for will come from Zion (mi-Tsiyyon), God's chosen dwelling on earth. The phrase be-shuv YHVH shevut ammo ('when the LORD restores the fortunes of his people') uses the idiom shuv shevut, which may mean 'to restore fortunes' or 'to return the captivity.' Either way, it points to comprehensive reversal — everything that has been lost, stolen, corrupted will be restored. The final line names Israel twice, by both names: Jacob (the wrestling, striving ancestor) will rejoice (yagel), and Israel (the name of divine encounter) will be glad (yismach). The doubled naming encompasses the nation's full identity — both its struggle and its election.
translator_notes: The phrase shuv shevut (or shivat) is debated: does it mean 'restore fortunes' (from shuv, 'to return') or 'return captivity' (from shavah, 'to take captive')? Both meanings work theologically — God either restores what was lost or brings back those who were taken away. The broader sense of total restoration is likely intended.

key_terms.note: Here yeshu'ah refers not to personal rescue but to national deliverance — the salvation of Israel as a people. The cry for salvation from Zion locates God's saving action in the place where his presence dwells among his people.
